coredata.dc:description: Polymer-based sensors, particularly those derived from renewable polymers, are gaining attention for their superior properties compared to organic small molecules. However, their complex preparation and poor, uncontrollable sensitivity have hindered further development. Herein, cellulose-based polymer photoluminescence (PL) chemosensors were fabricated using a straightforward and adjustable strategy. Specifically, water-soluble cellulose acetoacetate (CAA) was used as the substance for the in-situ synthesis of 1,4-dihydropyridine (DHPs) fluorescent rings on cellulose chains via a catalyst-free, room-temperature Hantzsch reaction. Benefiting from the synergetic through-space conjugation of DHPs rings and semi-rigid cellulose chains with heteroatoms, the sensors exhibit bright and stable PL properties. Based on this performance, the cellulose-based sensor excels in the specific recognition of Fe3+ in aqueous systems, showing exceptional selectivity, stability, and anti-interference performance due to the synergy between the inner filter effect (IFE) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). Theoretical calculations confirm the role of the extended π-conjugated structure at the DHPs-4 position in modulating the sensor sensitivity, achieving a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM. Furthermore, the versatility of the Hantzsch reaction shows the potential of this strategy for developing a new generation of biomass-based polymer portable sensors for real-time and on-site detection.

head.ce:title: Cellulose-based fluorescent chemosensor with controllable sensitivity for Fe3+ detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer-based sensors, particularly those derived from renewable polymers, are gaining attention for their superior properties compared to organic small molecules. However, their complex preparation and poor, uncontrollable sensitivity have hindered further development. Herein, cellulose-based polymer photoluminescence (PL) chemosensors were fabricated using a straightforward and adjustable strategy. Specifically, water-soluble cellulose acetoacetate (CAA) was used as the substance for the in-situ synthesis of 1,4-dihydropyridine (DHPs) fluorescent rings on cellulose chains via a catalyst-free, room-temperature Hantzsch reaction. Benefiting from the synergetic through-space conjugation of DHPs rings and semi-rigid cellulose chains with heteroatoms, the sensors exhibit bright and stable PL properties. Based on this performance, the cellulose-based sensor excels in the specific recognition of Fe3+ in aqueous systems, showing exceptional selectivity, stability, and anti-interference performance due to the synergy between the inner filter effect (IFE) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). Theoretical calculations confirm the role of the extended π-conjugated structure at the DHPs-4 position in modulating the sensor sensitivity, achieving a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.48 μM. Furthermore, the versatility of the Hantzsch reaction shows the potential of this strategy for developing a new generation of biomass-based polymer portable sensors for real-time and on-site detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the second most abundant metal element, iron plays a crucial role in various pathological and physiological processes, including oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and DNA or RNA synthesis, while also being extensively utilized in industrial production (Carter et al., 2014; Galaris et al., 2008; Huang et al., 2020). However, improperly treated industrial wastewater containing high levels of Fe3+ can foster the growth of harmful bacteria and cause environmental pollution (Liu, Diao, et al., 2017). Moreover, excessive Fe3+ in the human body can induce cell homeostasis disorders, leading to numerous physiological diseases and even threatening lives (Narayanaswamy & Govindaraju, 2012). Conversely, Fe3+ deficiency can cause anemia, reduced immune function, growth retardation, and other health issues. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a sensitive, specific, and convenient method to detect Fe3+ in both environmental and biological contexts.

Currently, among the various analytical techniques available for Fe3+, photoluminescence (PL) sensing technique is considered to be the most promising method due to its advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity, and relatively low cost (Guo et al., 2015; Liu, Chen, et al., 2020; Liu, Zhang, et al., 2020). The detection method of the PL sensors primarily depends on the luminescent responses, such as turn-off, turn-on, or ratiometric color change, etc. (Ravichandiran et al., 2020; Sahoo et al., 2012). To date, numerous advanced fluorescent materials have been employed to construct PL sensors for Fe3+ detection, including small organic fluorescent molecules, carbon quantum dots (Atchudan et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2015), metal nanoclusters (Huang et al., 2016), metal-organic frameworks (Liu et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2019; Sha & Yan, 2021) aggregation-induced emission (AIE) materials and so forth (Lin et al., 2019). Compared to organic small molecules, polymer-based luminescent sensors, particularly those derived from renewable polymers, have received more attention due to their advantages in signal amplification, processability, and environmental friendliness (Fan et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2020).

Cellulose, the most abundant natural renewable resource in the world, presents an ideal scaffold for metal ion sensing due to its easily modifiable ability, nontoxicity, and cost-effectiveness. Moreover, owing to the through-space conjugation interactions as well as conformation rigidification between intra- and intermolecular interactions, cellulose is a typical clusterization-triggered emission (CTE) polymer. However, its inherent weak fluorescence properties are insufficient for metal detection. To improve the recognition performance of cellulose-based metal ion sensors, a variety of physical doping with nanomaterials and chemical modification strategies have been explored. Because the chemical modification connects the luminescent molecule with the cellulose chain through covalent bonds, it offers superior performance stability compared to physical doping. At present, many cellulose-based sensors for Fe3+ detection have been synthesized through chemical modification strategies. For example, Xu et al. (Nawaz et al., 2022) designed and synthesized a multifunctional fluorescent material by grating 5-amino-2,3-dihydrophthalazine-1,4-dione onto cellulose chains. The obtained material exhibited outstanding performance in detection and removal of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions. Similarly, Cheng et al. (Ma & Cheng, 2023) first prepared a cellulose-based macromolecules reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (RAFT) reagent by reacting chloro-cellulose with CS2 and 2-mercaptobenzothiazole. Then the fluorescent monomers bearing -C=C and naphthalimide, and methacrylic acid were grafted onto the cellulose main chain through RAFT polymerization, resulting in a material capable of specifically detecting Fe3+ in water. Although these sensors have demonstrated excellent performances in recognizing Fe3+, their preparation processes are usually cumbersome and involve extensive use of organic solvents. Furthermore, most cellulose-based sensors for metal ion detection are limited to use in organic solvents, with sensitivity and selectivity requiring further improvement. Consequently, it is still a challenge to develop a facile and green cellulose-based fluorescent chemosensor with excellent optical performance, sensitivity, and specificity to detect Fe3+ in water.

1,4-dihydropyridines (DHPs) are classical nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds, which can be effectively produced through the multicomponent Hantzsch reaction consisting of β-ketoesters, aldehyde, and ammonia (Shen et al., 2009). The material of synthesized via the Hantzsch reaction exhibits outstanding AIE properties, making them highly promising for identification applications (Liu et al., 2021). Our previous studies demonstrated that cellulose derivatives with excellent PL and UV resistance can be prepared by the heterogeneous Hantzsch reaction, highlighting their potential in anti-counterfeiting and UV shielding applications (Liu et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2023; Qiu et al., 2024). However, studies on homogeneous Hnatzsch reaction based on cellulose matrix and biomass-based polymers containing DHP fluorescent rings for ion detection are rarely reported.

Herein, the cellulose-based polymer PL chemosensor with controllable sensitivity for specific detection of Fe3+ in aqueous systems was proposed through a simple and chemically adjustable multicomponent catalyst-free Hantzsch reaction. Firstly, water-soluble cellulose acetoacetate (CAA) with active acetoacetyl groups was prepared by transesterification reaction with t-BAA in ionic liquid (IL). After regeneration, CAA participated in the Hantzsch reaction at room temperature together with ammonium acetate and aldehyde to form the DHPs fluorescent rings on the cellulose chains in the aqueous system. As one of the simple DHPs models, the structures and PL properties of the CAA-FA (cellulose acetoacetate-formaldehyde) chemosensor were comprehensively investigated, and its luminous mechanism was further interpreted. Furthermore, it exhibited excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and anti-interference ability for Fe3+ recognition. Interestingly, given the universality of the Hantzsch reaction, the sensitivity of the resulting CAA-DHPs can be regulated by screening the structure of the conjugate ring of aldehyde components. Particularly, the CAA-DHPs sensor with varying sensitivity can be designed according to different application scenarios, which enhances their versatility and applicability in various fields. Additionally, given the diversity of cellulose forms, the strategy proposed in this work is expected to stimulate the development of more PL polymer portable sensors.

Coniferous wood pulp was supplied from Shandong Daoxin New Material Co., LTD (Qingdao, China; DP = 424) and dried at 100 °C for 3 h in a vacuum oven. 1-allyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (AMIMCl) was obtained from the Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.9 %), tert-butyl acetoacetate (t-BAA, 95 %), formaldehyde (FA, 37 wt%), benzaldehyde (BA, 98.5 %), naphthaldehyde (NA, 95 %), iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99 %), lithium chloride anhydrous (LiCl, 99.0 %), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 99 %), terbium chloride hexahydrate (TbCl3·6H2O, 99.9 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5 %), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99 %), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 99.5 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), manganese chloride (MnCl2, 99.99 %), iron dichloride (FeCl2, 99.5 %), aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99.99 %), mercury nitrate monohydrate (HgN2O6·H2O), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3, 98.0 %), sodium carbonate anhydrous (Na2CO3, 99.5 %), sodium sulfate anhydrous (Na2SO4, 99 %), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99 %), sodium iodide (NaI, 99.5 %), monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4, 99.9 %) were purchased from Mackin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade without further purification.

CAA (degree of substitution (DS) = 0.85, Fig. S2) was obtained from dried coniferous wood pulp by homogeneous transesterification with t-BAA in ionic liquid at 110 °C for 2 h (molar ratio: anhydroglucose unit (AGU) molar/t-BAA = 1/4) (Qiu et al., 2023). The DS of CAA was calculated according to the following equation:
DS =
I 1

Where I
1 was the integration area of the methyl group of acetoacetate and I
2 was the integration area of anhydroglucose ring of CAA.

Then, 200 mg NH4OAc and one equivalent FA were added to 10 g CAA aqueous solution (1 wt%). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for the Hantzsch reaction (Scheme S1). After that, the resulting material (CAA-FA powder) was successfully obtained through washing and freeze-drying technology. The CAA-FA sensor was prepared by dissolving CAA-FA powders in water.

Notably, other fluorescent sensors were also prepared with different aldehydes (BA, NA) instead of FA according to the above-mentioned procedure. The yield for each reaction was calculated by the following formula:
The yields of CAA-FA, CAA-BA, and CAA-NA were 66.44 %, 70.76 % and 54.43 %, respectively.

The fluorescent sensor was fabricated into a 0.20 mg/mL dispersion solution using distilled water. The necessary volumes of fluorescent sensor and 10 mM FeCl3 aqueous solution were added to a range of 3 mL centrifuge tubes in order to obtain the mixtures containing different concentrations of Fe3+. After mixing for 5 min in a shaker, the fluorescence intensity (λem = 475 nm) was recorded under 370 nm excitation (5 nm band-slits). The other fluorescent sensors with different aldehydes were also studied by the above-mentioned procedure. In addition, a series of metal cations (Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ga2+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Tb3+, Zr4+, Na+) and various anions (Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−) were added to this system as interference to replace Fe3+ standard solutions or both to demonstrate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ recognition, and the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm was recorded under 370 nm excitation (5 nm band-slits). The stability (λem = 475 nm) of the fluorescent sensor at different pH solutions was investigated by detecting the fluorescence intensity of each corresponding solution at λex = 370 nm. The pH of the solution was adjusted using 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M HCl solution. Moreover, all experiments were repeated three times to ensure the reliability of the experimental results and performed without adjusting the pH value except for special requirements.

The degree of polymerization (DP) of bleached coniferous wood pulp was estimated based on its intrinsic viscosity at 25 °C (Fang et al., 2020). In brief, dried bleached coniferous wood pulp was stirred in 0.5 mol/L copper ethylenediamine at 25 °C for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution. Intrinsic viscosities of the solutions were measured by using a Cannon-Fenske capillary viscometer. Then these values were converted to [η]*ρ values by using the Mark-Houwink-Sakurada equation. DP0.905 = 0.75 [η], where [η] is intrinsic viscosity, and ρ is pulp concentration. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were determined on an NMR spectrometer (AVANCE III HD 600, Switzerland) in D2O.

Elemental analysis (Elementar vario EL cube, Germany) was conducted to obtain the DS of CAA-DHPs according to the following equation (suppose a DHPs ring was formed between two glucose units) (Missoum et al., 2012; Peng et al., 2023):
DS =

CAA-FA = 235.45 g/mol, M

CAA-NA = 305.58 g/mol).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was measured by the KBr-disk method on Nicolet IS50-Nicolet Continuum (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). 13C solid NMR spectra were obtained on a solid-state NMR spectrometer (Bruker 400 M, Germany). (X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. X-ray diffraction data (XRD, X'pert Powder, PANalytical) was carried out to test the crystallinity of samples with a conventional wide-angle from 5°-60° at a scanning speed of 12°/min. Fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-7000 molecular fluorometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The geometry of the CAA derivative ground state was optimized using the B3LYP /def2-SVP level. The stable spatial structure of CAA-DHPs and Fe3+ was optimized by using the B3LYP/GENECP level. For C, H, O, and N the def2-SVP basis set was used and the SDD basis set was used for Fe3+. Grimme's D3BJ dispersion correction was applied to correct the weak interaction to improve calculation accuracy. The excited states calculations were conducted using time-dependent functional theory (TD-DFT) with the PE0 method and def2-TZVP basis set at the optimized ground state geometry. The Polarizable Continuum Model (PCM) implicit solvation model was used to interpret the solvation effect of water. All calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 package. The frontier molecular orbitals were presented through the Visual Molecular Dynamic (VMD) program in combination with the Multiwfn program (Lu & Chen, 2012).

Fig. 1 illustrates the procedure for the preparation of cellulose-based polymer sensors containing DHPs ring through a one-pot Hantzsch reaction. Based on previous reports, the water solubility of CAA can be regulated by varying the DS of acetoacetyl groups (Wu et al., 2020). Hence, to ensure that the resulting chemical sensor can detect targets in water, hydrosoluble CAA was selected as the main substrate. Subsequently, the cellulose-based fluorescent material was synthesized in a water system via the Hantzsch reaction at ambient temperature without any catalyst (Scheme S1). Finally, the obtained lyophilized powder was dispersed in water to fabricate the cellulose-based fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection. Subsequently, CAA-FA was used as a model to comprehensively study its structure, luminescent properties, and specific recognition mechanisms. As shown in Fig. 1, the CAA-FA powder exhibits a slight yellow color in the visible region, while emitting azure fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light (365 nm). Similarly, the CAA-FA aqueous solution displays the same phenomenon (Fig. S1).

FT-IR and 1H NMR were employed to confirm the successful esterification of cellulose. As shown in Fig. 2a and Fig. S2, the characteristic peaks observed at 1705 cm−1 and 1750 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum, along with signals at 2.2 ppm and 3.6 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum were attributed to the acetoacetyl groups on cellulose chains, suggesting the successful synthesis of hydrosoluble CAA (Qiu et al., 2023). For the CAA-FA sample, the appearance of new peaks at 1655 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 was ascribed to the CC and NH bonds of the DHPs ring. Additionally, in the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S3), the carbon signals at 205.1 ppm, 168.7 ppm, 55–110 ppm, 50.4 ppm, 30.1 ppm corresponded to keto carbonyl, acetoacetate ester, cellulose skeleton, methylene and methyl of acetoacetyl groups (Wang et al., 2023). The successful formation of the DHPs was further demonstrated by the signals of olefin carbon (141.9 ppm, 23.7 ppm) and secondary carbon (23.5 ppm). In addition, compared to CAA, the XPS full scan spectrum of CAA-FA in Fig. 2b exhibits a new peak at 400 eV, indicating the presence of N 1 s. The major peaks at 400.1 eV and 399.4 eV in deconvolution N 1 s spectra of CAA-FA sample were attributed to C-N-C and NH bonds belonging to the DHP ring, respectively (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the peak of CN (287.9 eV), C-O-C (533.2 eV), and CO (532.4 eV) were observed in the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s (Fig. S4a, b). These results further confirmed the successful fabrication of the CAA-FA via the Hantzsch reaction at room temperature. Furthermore, the DS of DHPs in the CAA-FA derivative sample was determined to be 0.41 according to elemental analysis (Table S1).

Compared to traditional AIE polymers, the CAA-FA polymer is free of classical large π-conjugates. However, it can exhibit bright blue fluorescence in both solid and aqueous solutions. In an aqueous solution, CAA-FA displays a broad emission range from 400 nm to 600 nm with a maximum emission wavelength of 475 nm under 370 nm excitation (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the CAA-FA solutions with different concentrations appear colorless or yellowish under sunlight, whereas emitting bright blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. S6a). Furthermore, their fluorescence intensity increases with the increasing of concentration accompanied by a slight redshift, as confirmed by Fig. S6b. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the higher concentrations favor the formation of larger clusters with conformation rigidification (Ren et al., 2022), suggesting the presence of excimer at higher concentrations. Moreover, CAA-FA also exhibits an excitation-wavelength-dependent bathochromic shift at longer excitation (Fig. 2f), confirming the presence of multiple emissive species with different effective through-space conjugation of the DHPs grafted to the cellulose backbone. These results demonstrated that CAA-FA possessed distinctive CTE properties.

The excellent fluorescent properties of CAA-FA were attributed to the combined effects of in-situ formed heterocyclic DHPs ring within the cellulose skeleton and semi-rigid cellulose chains containing heteroatoms (Xu et al., 2022). Firstly, as we previously reported, CAA contains acetoacetyl groups, hydroxy groups, and ether groups, whose clustering promotes efficient trough-space electron delocalization. Consequently, CAA solutions generate radiative emission with rigidified conformations when can be excited. The excitation and emission peaks are located at 340 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. S5). In addition, the formation of the DHPs ring further enhances the electronic excitation of the system. As shown in the excitation spectra of CAA-FA (Fig. 2d), owing to the absence of the large conjugated structures, CAA-FA shows only a 220 nm peak from π-π* in the UV adsorption spectrum. Previous studies have demonstrated the existence of absorption peaks above 300 nm from n-π*, although these transitions are typically forbidden (Lee et al., 2013; Liu, Wang, et al., 2017) As a result, this band is undetectable in low-sensitivity UV absorption spectra but can be found in hypersensitive excitation measurement. This explains that the significant differences between the adsorption and excitation spectra of CAA-FA in the longer wavelength range. Furthermore, theoretical calculation of the UV adsorption spectrum of the CAA-FA dimer also confirmed this conclusion (Fig. S6c). When a CAA-FA molecule in the singlet electronic ground state (S0) is excited to the higher-laying excited state (Sn) after absorbing energy, the Sn state is extremely short-lived due to the ultrafast relaxation through internal conversion (IC) and vibrational relaxation (VR) to the lowest singlet excited state (S1). The effective energy of S1 is then dissipated in the form of fluorescence (Fig. S6d) (Shen et al., 2023). When CAA-FA molecules aggregate, intra- and intermolecular interactions of heteroatoms in cellulose chains and heterocyclic DHPs ring, along with π electrons and lone pair electrons overlap, create an effective through-space electronic communication channel (Li et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). This facilitates efficient clusterization and prolongs electron delocalization. In addition, the heterocyclic DHPs ring grafted onto the cellulose chains through crosslinking further restricts fluorophore movement and stiffens the molecular conformation, inhibiting non-radiative transition. Therefore, the CAA-FA can emit bright fluorescence in both the aggregated state and high-concentration solutions.

To gain further insight into the electronic states of the CAA-FA molecule (Fig. S7), theoretical calculations of its electronic structure and energy levels were conducted. As shown in Fig. 2e, the highest occupied orbitals (HOMO) and lowest occupied orbitals (LUMO) of the molecule are predominantly located on the DHPs ring. Most of the intramolecular electrons are excited from the nitrogen atom and the olefinic carbons that link ester to the terminal olefinic carbons adjacent to the nitrogen atom, exhibiting a typical local excitation characteristic (Fan et al., 2018). This excitation process significantly reduces the energy gap between HOMO and LUMO, resulting in fluorescence emission. Moreover, after the introduction of the DHPs ring, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of the CAA-FA excited state is significantly reduced compared to CAA, confirming the generation of through-space conjugation (TSC) (Fig. S8). Additionally, as shown in Fig. S9, the decrease in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of the molecule from dimer to tetramer of CAA-FA reveals that cellulose aggregation also promotes the overlapping of the (n, π) and (π, π), further facilitating the formation of TSC. Therefore, the system exhibited excellent cluster luminescence. All these results further demonstrated the fluorescent properties of CAA-FA originated from the synergistic effects of the semi-rigid cellulose chains with heteroatoms and the heterocyclic DHPs ring.

Notably, pH has an impact on the sensitivity and selectivity of the chemical sensor for metal ions in environmental and biological systems. Therefore, the stability of fluorescence intensity at different pH levels was investigated (Miao et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. S10, pH variations have no significant effect on fluorescence intensity. Given excellent optical properties and stability, the CAA-FA aqueous solution was used as a PL sensor to systematically investigate the effects of various metal cations on its fluorescent properties. Initially, equal volumes of the prepared solutions containing different metal ions with the same concentration were added to 0.20 mg/mL of the CAA-FA sensor at ambient temperature. The fluorescence of the resulting metal ions-introduced CAA-FA sensor was then examined. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, most metal ions, including Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ga2+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Tb3+, Zr4+, and Na+, did not significantly quench the fluorescence. However, the CAA-FA sensor containing Fe3+ was quenched, and the mixture solution presented a yellowish color under sunlight. To further explore the anti-interference and specific detection of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm was recorded in Fig. 3b for three sets of experiments involving the CAA-FA (blank, orange), the CAA-FA with metal ions (blank + Mn+, green), and the CAA-FA with specified metal ions in addition to Fe3+ (blank + Mn+ + Fe3+). As depicted in Fig. 3b, the presence of other metal ions did not interfere with the quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on the CAA-FA sensor. The influence of different anions on the interference of Fe3+ detected by the CAA-FA sensor is also negligible (Fig. 3c), confirming that the CAA-FA sensor has a specific affinity for Fe3+. In addition, the effect of pH on the degree of quenching was also implemented. As shown in Fig. 3d, Fe3+ exhibits similar quenching effects on the CAA-FA sensor over a wide pH range, suggesting that the CAA-FA sensor can not only maintain fluorescence stability at pH values of 3–12, but also achieve detection under extreme conditions. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S11, the fluorescence intensity of CAA-FA at 475 nm decreases gradually with increasing Fe3+ concentration, suggesting that CAA-FA has promising applications in metal ion detection.

Subsequently, the Stern-Volmer model was used to further study PL quenching (Yue et al., 2019):
(1)
F 0
/ F − 1 =
K sv
Q
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the CAA-FA sensor in the absence and the presence of Fe3+, respectively; Ksv is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+.

As depicted in Fig. 3e, good linear relations of F0/F-1 and [Q] are observed (F0/F-1 = 0.00943 [Q] + 0.001194), suggesting that the Stern−Volmer quenching constant KSV is 0.00943 μM−1. Moreover, the fluorescent limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 3.56 μM using the following formula (Hou et al., 2022).
(2)
LOD = 3 /
K sv
n = 10
Where σ and k represent the standard deviation of the blank fluorescent signal and the slope of the linear calibration plot, respectively (Yu et al., 2023).

The “standards for drinking water quality” clearly state that the iron content in water should not exceed 0.3 mg/mL (World Health Organization, 2004). Fortunately, the detection limit of the CAA-FA sensor was found to be much lower than this standard value, indicating the CAA-FA sensor can be applied to the detection of Fe3+ in drinking water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible quenching mechanism and regulation of sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ by CAA-FA may be attributed to the combination of multiple mechanisms. To understand its fluorescence quenching mechanism, a series of experiments was carried out. Firstly, the UV absorption spectrum of Fe3+ ions was compared with the fluorescence spectrum of CAA-FA. It can be observed from Fig. S12 that Fe3+ has a strong absorption spectrum with a wide range from 200 to 380 nm, which partially overlaps with the excitation spectrum of CAA-FA, enabling the generation of inner filter effect (IFE) between CAA-FA and Fe3+ (Chen et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime assay was performed to further study the fluorescence mechanism. As shown in Fig. 4f, after adding Fe3+, the average fluorescence lifetime changes from 4.22 ns to 3.66 ns, suggesting that the fluorescence quenching process is not only attributable to IFE.

Considering that paramagnetic Fe3+ has unfilled d orbitals and possesses a greater affinity for CAA-FA than other metal ions, Fe3+ can may coordinate with the CAA-FA sensor to form a stable non-fluorescent complex. Moreover, Fe3+ probably participates in electron/energy transfer processes, opening a non-radioactive deactivation channel, resulting in the distinct fluorescence quenching of the CAA-FA sensor (Cui et al., 2020; Nawaz et al., 2018). To verify these assumptions, UV–vis adsorption spectra were utilized to investigate the optical response of the CAA-FA sensor toward different metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4a, the CAA-FA sensor exhibits a strong absorption spectrum with a wide range from 200 to 450 nm after the addition of Fe3+ while no change is observed after introducing other metal ions. This variation was attributed to the coordination of the CAA-FA sensor and Fe3+ ions, demonstrating the formation of a complex between CAA-FA and Fe3+. To clearly understand the coordination between Fe3+ and the CAA-FA sensor, the FT-IR and XPS techniques were employed to characterize the structural changes before and after the addition of Fe3+. After dipping in Fe3+, the peaks attributed to CO and CN are significantly changed (Fig. 4b), indicating that carbonyl groups and NH groups are involved in the chelation of Fe3+. The XPS spectrum of CAA-FA-Fe displays a new peak related to Fe2p (Fig. 4c), further demonstrating the existence of interaction between Fe3+ and CAA-FA substance. Moreover, Fig. 4d and Fig. 4e show the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s and N1s before and after the addition of Fe3+. Compared to the original spectrum, the positions of the C-O-C, CO, C-N-C, and NH bonds peak change slightly after coordination with Fe3+. All these changes confirmed that Fe3+ had special coordination/chelation interactions with N and O containing functional groups of the prepared CAA-FA sensor (Chang et al., 2021), affecting the electron density distribution of the system (Mei et al., 2022).

To better demonstrate the influence of Fe3+ on the electron distribution of the CAA-FA system, DFT calculations were carried out to investigate the frontier molecular orbitals during the quenching process. After the chelation of CAA-FA with Fe3+, the charge of the HOMO orbital is transferred from the heterocyclic DHPs ring to the metal ion due to the strong electron-withdrawing ability of Fe3+ (Fig. 4g), indicating the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). In addition, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of the complex is dramatically smaller (Fig. 4g), which is beneficial to Fe3+ recognition. Moreover, the binding energies between CAA-FA and Fe3+ are negative (Table S2), further indicating a strong affinity between the two (Zhan et al., 2020). The local excitation process of the heterocyclic DHPs ring was disrupted by the presence of Fe3+, thereby quenching the fluorescence of the system. In summary, after the addition of Fe3+, a stable complex was formed, resulting in the IFE and the unfavorable ICT process, which synergistically quenched the fluorescence of the CAA-FA sensor.

It has been reported that the introduction of a benzene ring structure at the 4-position of the DHPs ring can decrease the energy gap (HOMO-LUMO) of the system and enhance charge transfer excitation (Fan et al., 2018). Moreover, the introduction of conjugated rings can also enhance UV absorption, thereby facilitating electronic excitation within the system. These factors are conducive to CAA-DHPs forming a stable complex with Fe3+ and IFE, which could improve the sensitivity of the sensor. Therefore, BA with one benzene ring and NA with two benzene rings were expected to be used to regulate the sensitivity of the cellulose-based polymer sensor. The 13C NMR, XPS, and FT-IR spectra of CAA-BA and CAA-NA were shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S13, which confirmed the successful formation of the DHPs ring with an extended conjugated structure. Moreover, the DS of different CAA-DHPs was calculated from the elemental analysis (Table S1).

The frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of CAA derivatives with different substituents at the 4-position of the DHPs ring are shown in Fig. 5a. The energy gap between HOMO and LUMO decreased with the extension of the DHP-4 conjugation, which demonstrated that the introduction of benzene rings was favorable for enhancing the electron cloud density and reactivity of the system. Additionally, compared to the CAA-FA samples, new absorption bands attributed to benzene and naphthalene rings appear in the UV absorption spectra of CAA-BA and CAA-NA, respectively (Fig. S14a). Moreover, as shown in Fig. S14b, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ overlaps the excitation spectra of CAA-BA and CAA-NA to a greater extent than CAA-FA, revealing the generation of a stronger IFE. Subsequently, the sensitivity of these sensors was investigated using the Stern-Volmer model. As presented in Fig. 5b-c, the LOD of the sensor also decreased as the degree of conjugation increased at the 4-position. The minimum LOD can reach 0.48 μM for the CAA-NA sensor, which is an order of magnitude higher than that of the CAA-FA sensor (3.56 μM). This suggested that CAA-NA molecules were more likely to form more stable chelates with Fe3+ in the ground state, resulting in fluorescence quenching. This further confirmed that the sensitivity of the sensor can be regulated by changing the structure of the aldehyde component. Table S3 shows that the LOD of sensing for the resulting CAA-DHPs is comparable to some biomass-based Fe3+ sensors reported in previous works (Gupta et al., 2023; Liu, Chen, et al., 2020; Liu, Zhang, et al., 2020; Ma & Cheng, 2023; Nawaz et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Yildirim et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2024). However, the preparation process of the CAA-DHPs sensor was carried out in the water phase, and was simple and environmentally friendly. Most importantly, the sensitivity of our prepared CAA-DHPs can be controlled to meet different application scenarios. This work demonstrates that the Hantzsch reaction provides a broad platform for the preparation of cellulose-based PL chemosensors for detection applications. Moreover, this strategy has the potential to facilitate the development of biomass-based CTE polymer sensors.

In summary, a sensitivity-controllable PL chemosensor based on cellulose was synthesized via a facile and efficient multicomponent Hantzsch reaction at room temperature for the specific detection of Fe3+ in aqueous system. The stable and excellent PL properties of the sensor were primarily attributed to the synergetic through-space conjugation effect of the heterocyclic DHPs ring and semi-rigid cellulose chains with heteroatoms. Especially, the CAA-FA model sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and anti-interference performance for Fe3+ detection due to the generation of the IFE and ICT process. Based on this characteristic, a novel strategy was developed to regulate the sensitivity of the sensor, achieving an order-of- magnitude improvement in LOD with the resulting CAA-NA model sensor. Moreover, given the easy modification of hydroxy groups and general adaptation of the Hantzsch reaction, the method will be expected to facilitate the progress of more sensitive and portable biomass-based sensors, paving the way for real-time and on-site detection. Furthermore, the strategy presented in this work will not only expand the development of sustainable and eco-friendly sensing materials based on renewable biomass resources but also encourage further exploration of biomass-based CTE polymers in the detection field.
